summary: I was born and raised in Somalia, specifically in its capital city, Mogadishu. Somalia has a unique culture with a shared language, religion, and traditions among its people. Despite the political instability and civil war that began in the 1990s, Somalia remains a place close to my heart, especially because of its traditional clothing like the ma'awes that my father first bought me. Living there for the first fifteen years of my life shaped who I am today.

Somalia is located in the Horn of Africa, a region that has seen a lot of political instability over the past twenty-five years. Being part of this region means that my homeland shares many challenges and cultural ties with neighboring countries. The Horn of Africa's geography and climate influenced the way of life in Somalia, including the clothing we wear to adapt to the heat. My experiences growing up in this region have deeply influenced my identity and perspective.

The Somali Civil War started in the 1990s and drastically changed my life and the lives of many Somalis. In 2008, as the situation worsened, I had to leave Somalia and move to Uganda, leaving my family behind. This war created a lot of hardship, forcing people like me to seek safety and stability elsewhere. It was during this time that I faced many challenges living alone in Uganda without enough resources or support.

Mogadishu is the capital city of Somalia where I was born and spent the first fifteen years of my life. It was my home before the civil war forced me to leave. Growing up in Mogadishu, I experienced the rich Somali culture firsthand, including traditions like wearing the ma'awes. Despite the conflict that affected the city, Mogadishu remains a significant part of my personal history and identity.

In 2008, I moved to Uganda to escape the worsening situation in Somalia during the civil war. I lived there alone for five years, which was very difficult because I was separated from my family and struggled with limited food, money, and health care. My neighbors who traveled with me helped me during this time. Uganda was a place of survival and transition before I eventually moved to the United States for better opportunities.

After five years in Uganda, I moved to the United States to pursue high-quality education and a better life. I settled in Minnesota, knowing that the culture and climate here are very different from Somalia. For example, I cannot wear my traditional ma'awes in public because of the cold weather and cultural differences. Still, America represents hope and opportunity for me as I try to build a new future.

I live in Minnesota now, and the weather here is very different from Somalia's hot climate. Because of this cold weather, I can't wear my traditional Somali garment, the ma'awes, outside. Also, the American culture here doesn't really accept wearing it publicly. Minnesota is where I am trying to create a new life, but I still hold on to my Somali traditions and hope to share them through things like starting a ma'awes clothing line.

Eid is an important celebration in Somali culture, and many Somali men wear the traditional ma'awes garment during this time. I remember wearing my ma'awes at Eid celebrations back home in Somalia, which connected me to my family and heritage. These celebrations are joyful moments that highlight our shared religion and traditions. Even now, I cherish those memories and the role Eid plays in keeping our culture alive.
